synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.9: Student behaviour intervention process]
As a team, we strive to work from a preventative perspective rather than reacting. When we intervene at an early stage, we can provide children with tools and strategies at a moment when they can learn them better. However, there are always times when we cannot stop inappropriate behaviours from happening.

In dealing with any inappropriate behaviours, our first task is to ensure that the child can understand why that behaviour was inappropriate and take responsibility.

As a staff, we treat all children with respect, and physical interventions are not used except in extreme cases where the safety of others is at stake.

We ask children to describe what happened, explain the impact of their action (if they are unable to do this), and understand why this is inappropriate. This discussion may happen immediately or after, depending on the child.

Our behaviour flowchart (see image below) is used to guide actions and interventions in a scalable order of minor and major incidents. A behaviour referral form is used to track behavioural incidents for minor, major, or unacceptable concerns. This will be designated by the person who witnesses the incident/behaviour. Each time a child fills in an incident form, this will be sent home via email to inform families. After 5 minor incidents or 3 major incidents occur, the school will contact the family to pick up the child, and they will have to remain home the following day. It is very important to keep parents informed and work together to offer the child help at both school and home. The Student Support Team is involved at this stage, and next steps for the parents will be agreed upon.

|   |   | Behaviour observation |   |   |   |   |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
|   |   | Teacher and the student fill-in the **INCIDENT REPORT** |   |   |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
|   |   | **STEP 1** Before you fill in an incident report, make sure you talk to other adults or other children present at that moment. Classify the behaviour. |   |   |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
| **Orange** (minor behaviour) Example: inappropriate verbal language. |   |   |   | **Red** (major behaviour) Example: Property damage/theft. |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
| **STEP 2** Natural consequences applied. - Talk to the child about the situation and the natural consequence that situation has, as well as share the expected behaviour. |   |   |   | **STEP 2** Natural consequences applied. - Talk to the child about the situation and the natural consequence that situation has, as well as share the expected behaviour. |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
| **STEP 3** Inform the Student Support Team & families. - Families sign the incident report at dismissal time or the teacher sends the incident report through email. - If you send an email to families, add your coordinator in it (Sheila or Lindsay). - The document will be filed in a folder that will be kept in the Coordinator office. |   |   |   | **STEP 3** Inform the Student Support Team & families. - Families sign the incident report at dismissal time or the teacher sends the incident report through email. - If you send an email to families, add your coordinator in it (Sheila or Lindsay). - The document will be filed in a folder that will be kept in the Coordinator office. |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
|   | **3 incident reports** |   |   |   | **2 incident reports** |   |
|   |   | **STEP 4 1 DAY AT HOME** Student Support Team call the family to communicate the consequence. The student needs to be picked up from school. The child needs to stay home 1 day as a consequence of his/her behaviour at school. |   |   |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
|   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
| **Note:** We could avoid the flowchart in case of an extreme behaviour such as physical aggression, or high-risk situation and go straight to STEP 4. Any measure applied should be approved by the Head Teacher. |   |   |   |   |   |   |

From a Positive Behaviour Support approach, it is recognised that reasons for inappropriate behaviours might require immediate action, including removing the child from the situation until a resolution can be calmly sought. This should be done especially if a child presents a danger to themselves or others. This should not be seen as a sanction nor treated as one. Children will never be left on their own in these occasions.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.13: Student removal from class policy]
Following the behaviour flowchart, if a child needs to be removed from the classroom because they present a danger to themselves or others, OR normal reminders are not working and they need to take a break, this should not be presented as a sanction but a normal flow of consequences resulting from their choices.

A choice is always presented to the child before removal from a class, situation, or location. He/she can choose to modify their behaviour to meet the expectations of the situation or can leave the place to take a break. When they are ready, they may come back into the situation, with a clear idea of what their next steps will be.

If a child has a special intervention plan, they may have this included as a strategy to help them self-regulate their behaviour.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.14: Staff immediate action inappropriate behavior]
Staff have the right to impose immediate action with children displaying inappropriate behaviour. These are always relevant to the particular child and involve the removal of short-term privileges or natural consequences established in the classroom by the children with the support of the guide. Examples of such actions might include:

*   Working on their own rather than with peers.
*   Loss of privilege (e.g., park-time, social lunch).
*   Restricted to work in a particular area.

A “classroom reflection” might or might not be filled in, depending on the severity of the situations.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.17: Physical restraint policy and incident reporting]
*   In special circumstances, we may use physical restraint such as holding or picking up a child. We ONLY ever do this to prevent physical injury to themselves, other children/adults, or damage to property. When it is practically possible, we let a child know what we are going to do beforehand.
*   Details of a serious incident of this nature are required to be catalogued. We use the “classroom reflection” tracker for this and describe in detail what happened in the ‘note’ section of the form. Details include what happened, who was there, what action was taken and by whom. Details of the incident are kept in the child's personal file. The parent is informed on the same day as per the requirements of the referral tracking form.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.20: Young Children Hurtful Behaviour Management]
Sometimes young children say hurtful things impulsively without considering what they are saying or the consequences. We use these as teachable moments and help them to manage their feelings in these moments or later, once they are calm. We help young children to learn to empathise through a variety of strategies including role play, modelling behaviour, and circle time. They also learn that they need to fix the problem, instead of just saying sorry.

When these behaviours become problematic, we involve parents to identify the cause and find a solution together. The main reasons for excessive hurtful behaviour usually include:

*   They do not feel securely attached to someone who can interpret and meet their needs: this may be at home or in the school setting.
*   Their parent or carer in the school setting does not have the requisite skills to respond appropriately, and consequently, negative patterns start emerging.
*   The child is exposed to aggressive behaviour at home or from peers at school.
*   In the case of suspected child abuse, we are required to report it to Social Services.
*   The child has a developmental condition that affects how they behave.

In meetings with the school psychologist, we will aim to provide a unified approach in dealing with these behaviours, both at home and in school.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:6.11.L8: Actions and Consequences]
Actions and Consequences**

The following sanctions may be applied in accordance with the school’s Behaviour Policy:

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:8.5: Pupil Search, Confiscation, Next Steps]
1.  **Searching**
    School staff can search a pupil for any item if the pupil agrees. Headteachers and staff have a statutory power to search pupils or their possessions, without consent, where they have reasonable grounds for suspecting that the pupil may have a prohibited item.

2.  **Confiscating**
    School staff can seize any prohibited item found as a result of a search. They can also seize any item they consider harmful or detrimental to school discipline.

3.  **Deciding on the Next Course of Action**
    From the outcome of the investigation, the school must decide on the level of risk the pupil poses in school. If the risk is clear and the intent is obvious, then a fixed-term exclusion and a disciplinary hearing may follow, or the decision to permanently exclude may be made (following necessary protocols, reporting to the police, social services, etc.). However, depending on the intent, the age of the child, and also the circumstances surrounding the incident or the contextual issues, it may be that a fixed-term exclusion is given, followed by a risk assessment/safety plan put in place following the pupil’s return to school.

    **Returning to School/Safety Planning**
    Safety planning is a positive way of supporting a child who may benefit from a planned approach to support or intervention following an incident where a weapon has been brought to school. Safety plans support the child by considering the behaviour behind the use of a weapon and planning ways to manage any identified risks/triggers and seek support from adults and peers. They are inclusive of parents and staff and are a planned intervention to support young people in feeling secure in the school/setting, helping young people identify behaviours that may leave them feeling anxious or at risk, and have strategies they can apply to keep themselves feeling safe.

    Each case will be judged on an individual basis.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.10: Child Protection Complaints Procedure]
Our complaints procedure will be followed when a pupil or parent raises a concern about poor practice towards a pupil that initially does not reach the threshold for child protection action. Poor practice examples include unfairly singling out a pupil or attempting to humiliate them, bullying or belittling a pupil, or discriminating against them in some way. Complaints are managed by the head teacher or other members of the senior leadership team. An explanation of the complaints procedure is included in the safeguarding information for parents and pupils. *All complaints will be dealt with within 28 days.*

Complaints from staff are dealt with under the school's complaints and disciplinary and grievance procedures.

Complaints which escalate into a child protection concern will automatically be managed under the school's child protection procedures.

Please also refer to the school's Complaints Policy for all staff and volunteers.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.16: Standards for Effective Child Protection Practice]
**Standards for Effective Child Protection Practice in Schools**

The school's child protection and safeguarding responsibilities are inspected under the ‘Quality of Leadership and Management' and 'Behaviour and Safety' judgements in Ofsted inspections. The following standards may assist schools in evaluating their practice. They should be used jointly by the Designated Safeguarding Lead to ensure the school is effective in safeguarding and child protection matters.

**In best practice, schools:**

1.  Have an ethos in which children feel secure, their viewpoints are valued, and they are encouraged to talk and are listened to.
2.  Provide suitable support and guidance so that pupils have a range of appropriate adults to whom they can turn if they are worried or in difficulties.
3.  Work with parents to build an understanding of the school's responsibilities to safeguard and promote the welfare of all children and a recognition that this may occasionally require children to be referred to investigative agencies as a constructive and helpful measure.
4.  Ensure all staff are able to identify children who may benefit from early help; provide coordinated offers of early help; and ensure that children receive the right help at the right time to address concerns and risks and prevent issues from escalating.
5.  Are vigilant in cases of suspected child abuse, recognising the signs and symptoms, have clear procedures whereby all members of staff report such cases to the Designated Safeguarding Lead or - in her/his absence - the deputy Designated Safeguarding Lead, and are aware of Local Authority and Local Safeguarding Children Board procedures so that information is passed on effectively to the relevant professionals.
6.  Monitor children who have been identified as in need of early help or at risk; maintain clear records of pupils' progress and welfare *in a secure place*; maintain sound policies on confidentiality; provide appropriate information to other professionals; and submit reports to and attend child protection conferences.
7.  Provide and support regular child protection training and updates for all school staff and ensure that Designated Safeguarding Leads attend refresher training every two years to ensure their skills and expertise are up to date; and ensure that targeted funding for this work is used solely for this purpose.
8.  Contribute to an inter-agency approach to safeguarding and child protection by developing effective and supportive liaison with other agencies.
9.  Use the curriculum to teach children about safeguarding and raise their awareness and build confidence so that pupils have a range of contacts and strategies to ensure their own protection and understand the importance of protecting others.
10. Provide clear policy statements for parents, staff, and children and young people on the school’s policies.
11. Have a clear understanding of the various types of bullying - face-to-face, online, physical, verbal, and indirect - and act promptly and firmly to combat it, making sure that pupils are aware of the school's position on this issue and who they can contact for support.
12. Take particular care that pupils with SEN in mainstream and special schools, who may be especially vulnerable to abuse, are supported effectively, with particular attention paid to ensuring that those with communication difficulties are enabled to express themselves to a member of staff with appropriate communication skills.
13. Have a clear policy about the handling of allegations of abuse by members of staff, ensuring that all staff are fully aware of the procedures and that they are followed correctly at all times, using the guidance set out in *Keeping Children Safe in Education 2024* and WSCB inter-agency child protection procedures.
14. Have a written whole school policy, which is produced, owned, and regularly reviewed by all school staff, considering the views of children and parents/carers, and which clearly outlines the school's position and positive action in respect of the aforementioned standards.
synthesis.raw_response:
If a student physically attacks a teacher, the school may avoid the standard behaviour flowchart and go straight to "STEP 4" [4.9]. This involves the Student Support Team contacting the family to communicate the consequence, and the student being picked up from school to stay home for one day [4.9].

Additionally, if the risk is clear and the intent is obvious, a fixed-term exclusion and a disciplinary hearing may follow, or the decision to permanently exclude may be made, which could involve reporting to the police and social services [8.5]. The student may also be removed from the situation if they present a danger to themselves or others [4.9, 4.13]. Any measure applied should be approved by the Head Teacher, and each case will be judged on an individual basis [4.9, 8.5].

synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.5: Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) Responsibilities]
The DSL:

*   Is a senior member of staff from the school’s leadership team and therefore has the status and authority within the school to carry out the duties of the post, including committing resources and supporting and directing other staff.
*   Takes lead responsibility for safeguarding and child protection in the school, which will not be delegated, although the activities of the DSL may be delegated to appropriately trained deputies.
*   Acts as a source of support and expertise to the school community.
*   Encourages a culture of listening to children and taking account of their wishes and feelings.
*   Is alert to the specific needs of children in need, those with special educational needs, looked-after children, and young carers.
*   Keeps detailed written records of all concerns, ensuring that such records are stored securely and flagged, but kept separate from, the pupil's general file.
*   Refers cases of suspected abuse to Children's Social Care or the Police as appropriate.
*   Notifies Children's Social Care if a child with a “Child protection plan” is absent for more than three days without explanation.
*   Ensures that, when a pupil leaves the school, all child protection records are passed to the new school (separately from the main pupil file and ensuring secure transit) and confirmation of receipt is obtained. If the child is the subject of an open case to Children's Social Care, the pupil's social worker is also informed.
*   Co-ordinates the school's contribution to child protection plans as part of core groups, attending and actively participating in core group meetings.
*   Ensures that all staff sign to indicate that they have read and understood the child protection and safeguarding policy and Staff Behaviour Policy (Code of Conduct).
*   Ensures that the child protection and safeguarding policy and procedures are regularly reviewed and updated annually, working with the whole school community of pupils, parents, staff, and volunteers regarding this.
*   Keeps a record of staff attendance at child protection training.
*   Makes the child protection and safeguarding policy available publicly, i.e., on the school's website or by other means.
*   Ensures parents are aware of the school's role in safeguarding and that referrals about suspected abuse and neglect may be made.

**DSL 1:** Sheila Ortiz Morales - Head Teacher of Children’s House and Primary
**DSL 2:** Lindsay Kerr - Head Teacher of Secondary and A Levels

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.6: Deputy DSL Role and Responsibilities]
Is appropriately trained to the same level as the DSL and, in the absence of the DSL, carries out those functions necessary to ensure the ongoing safety and protection of pupils. In the event of the long-term absence of the DSL, the deputy will assume all of the functions above.

**Deputy DSL:**
Sheila Ortiz Morales - Head Teacher of Children’s House and Primary
Lindsay Kerr - Head Teacher of Secondary and A Levels

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.10: Child Protection Complaints Procedure]
Our complaints procedure will be followed when a pupil or parent raises a concern about poor practice towards a pupil that initially does not reach the threshold for child protection action. Poor practice examples include unfairly singling out a pupil or attempting to humiliate them, bullying or belittling a pupil, or discriminating against them in some way. Complaints are managed by the head teacher or other members of the senior leadership team. An explanation of the complaints procedure is included in the safeguarding information for parents and pupils. *All complaints will be dealt with within 28 days.*

Complaints from staff are dealt with under the school's complaints and disciplinary and grievance procedures.

Complaints which escalate into a child protection concern will automatically be managed under the school's child protection procedures.

Please also refer to the school's Complaints Policy for all staff and volunteers.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.5: Head Teachers' Behaviour Responsibility]
The Head Teachers have overall responsibility for behaviour at Imagine Montessori, which includes personal, social, and emotional development and any related issues arising within this framework.

The Head Teachers will strive to:

*   Keep up-to-date on any new legislation arising, including research in positive behaviour and instituting a Student Support Team, which will include the Head Teacher and school psychologist.
*   Keep staff up-to-date and offer professional development where required to assist them in the function of their roles and responsibilities within the school.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:4.20: Young Children Hurtful Behaviour Management]
Sometimes young children say hurtful things impulsively without considering what they are saying or the consequences. We use these as teachable moments and help them to manage their feelings in these moments or later, once they are calm. We help young children to learn to empathise through a variety of strategies including role play, modelling behaviour, and circle time. They also learn that they need to fix the problem, instead of just saying sorry.

When these behaviours become problematic, we involve parents to identify the cause and find a solution together. The main reasons for excessive hurtful behaviour usually include:

*   They do not feel securely attached to someone who can interpret and meet their needs: this may be at home or in the school setting.
*   Their parent or carer in the school setting does not have the requisite skills to respond appropriately, and consequently, negative patterns start emerging.
*   The child is exposed to aggressive behaviour at home or from peers at school.
*   In the case of suspected child abuse, we are required to report it to Social Services.
*   The child has a developmental condition that affects how they behave.

In meetings with the school psychologist, we will aim to provide a unified approach in dealing with these behaviours, both at home and in school.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:6.10: Bullying: Identifying, Reporting, and Actions]
Students and families should report any suspicion of bullying immediately to any of the designated people below:

*   Tutor
*   Key Stage Manager
*   Head teacher

Much of the behaviour which leads to both bullying and intimidation is difficult to observe or can be seen as innocent when it is not. It is therefore essential that staff listen carefully to what children say and watch for any signs that a child may be being intimidated. The staff must always pass on their concern to the other members of staff working with the child and report these concerns to the Head Teacher.

**If you witness any unacceptable behaviour, how you should act:**

1.  **If you feel you are being bullied.**
    Tell someone.
    If you feel you are being bullied, you can tell a member of staff and/or your parent/carer (anyone you feel safe to talk to). It is not weak to do this.
    If you cannot tell anyone at school, you can tell your parents.
    Friends of those who feel bullied should let staff know of bullying incidents.
    No one deserves to be bullied. You have a right for this not to happen to you.

2.  **If you are a pupil and witness bullying behaviour.**
    Support the child who you observed being bullied by offering your friendship and making it clear that, in your opinion, what is happening to them is unkind.
    Encourage them to speak out on their own behalf by confronting the child who was exhibiting the bullying behaviour, or with their permission, confront that child yourself. Accompany the child who you observed being bullied to a trusted adult or suggest that you speak to an adult on their behalf.

3.  **If you are a member of Staff.**
    Reassure and support the pupils involved.
    Advise them that you are required to pass details on to their Guide and/or to the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour (Key Stage Coordinator/Head Teacher).
    The named person will keep a log of all complaints or incidences of bullying and record the way in which they were dealt with.

4.  **If you are a Parent.**
    Bring the issue to the attention of any member of staff, or to the Head of School, or the named person responsible for supporting good behaviour.

---

[Section en_policy5_code_of_conduct:6.11.L37: Reporting and Review]
This may be reported to the local authority.

These are minimum sanctions. In very serious cases, it may be necessary to make a report to the Police or Social Services. However, it is the policy of the school to attempt to resolve such issues internally using our own disciplinary sanctions, unless the matter is of such gravity that a criminal prosecution is likely.

The staff will discuss this policy and its implications on their practice at least once every year. Where possible, parents will be invited to contribute to the school’s policy and procedures to prevent bullying. The records of bullying offences will be reviewed by the Management Team once a year at their ‘Dashboard Meeting’ at the end of each school year.

In parallel, we will tackle the issue within the year group/class.

Each incident will be discussed at the weekly staff meeting to inform the other members of staff about how it has been addressed and prevent further incidents.

```
synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.4: Safeguarding legislation and guidance]
**Independent Schools:**

Section 157 of the Education Act 2002 and the Education (Independent School Standards) Regulations 2014 require proprietors of independent schools to have arrangements to safeguard and promote the welfare of children who are pupils at the school.

*   The Teacher Standards 2012 state that teachers, including head teachers, must have regard for the need to safeguard pupils' well-being, in accordance with statutory provisions, and maintain public trust in the teaching profession as part of their professional duties.
*   The statutory guidance *Working Together to Safeguard Children* (DfE 2015) covers the legislative requirements and expectations on individual services (including schools) to safeguard and promote the welfare of children.
*   The statutory guidance *Keeping Children Safe in Education* (DFE 2016) is issued under Section 175 of the Education Act 2002, the Education (Independent School Standards) Regulations 2014, and the Non-Maintained Special Schools (England) Regulations 2015. Schools must have regard to this guidance when carrying out their duties to safeguard and promote the welfare of children. Unless otherwise stated, 'school' in this guidance means all schools, whether maintained, non-maintained, or independent, including academies and free schools, alternative provision academies, maintained nursery schools, and pupil referral units.
*   All staff must read Part One of *Keeping Children Safe in Education 2016*.
*   *What to do if you're worried a child is being abused 2015* - Advice for practitioners is non-statutory advice that helps practitioners (everyone who works with children) to identify abuse and neglect and take appropriate action. Staff can find a copy in the minutes book and policy folder (please sign).

Due to their day-to-day contact with pupils, staff in school are uniquely placed to observe changes in children's behaviour and the outward signs of abuse, neglect, exploitation, and radicalisation. Children may also turn to a trusted adult in school when they are in distress or at risk. It is vital that all school staff are alert to the signs of abuse, are approachable and trusted by pupils/students, listen actively to children, and understand the procedures for reporting their concerns. The school will act on identified concerns and will provide early help to prevent concerns from escalating.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.5: Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) Responsibilities]
The DSL:

*   Is a senior member of staff from the school’s leadership team and therefore has the status and authority within the school to carry out the duties of the post, including committing resources and supporting and directing other staff.
*   Takes lead responsibility for safeguarding and child protection in the school, which will not be delegated, although the activities of the DSL may be delegated to appropriately trained deputies.
*   Acts as a source of support and expertise to the school community.
*   Encourages a culture of listening to children and taking account of their wishes and feelings.
*   Is alert to the specific needs of children in need, those with special educational needs, looked-after children, and young carers.
*   Keeps detailed written records of all concerns, ensuring that such records are stored securely and flagged, but kept separate from, the pupil's general file.
*   Refers cases of suspected abuse to Children's Social Care or the Police as appropriate.
*   Notifies Children's Social Care if a child with a “Child protection plan” is absent for more than three days without explanation.
*   Ensures that, when a pupil leaves the school, all child protection records are passed to the new school (separately from the main pupil file and ensuring secure transit) and confirmation of receipt is obtained. If the child is the subject of an open case to Children's Social Care, the pupil's social worker is also informed.
*   Co-ordinates the school's contribution to child protection plans as part of core groups, attending and actively participating in core group meetings.
*   Ensures that all staff sign to indicate that they have read and understood the child protection and safeguarding policy and Staff Behaviour Policy (Code of Conduct).
*   Ensures that the child protection and safeguarding policy and procedures are regularly reviewed and updated annually, working with the whole school community of pupils, parents, staff, and volunteers regarding this.
*   Keeps a record of staff attendance at child protection training.
*   Makes the child protection and safeguarding policy available publicly, i.e., on the school's website or by other means.
*   Ensures parents are aware of the school's role in safeguarding and that referrals about suspected abuse and neglect may be made.

**DSL 1:** Sheila Ortiz Morales - Head Teacher of Children’s House and Primary
**DSL 2:** Lindsay Kerr - Head Teacher of Secondary and A Levels

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.8: Children Missing Education Procedures]
All children, regardless of their circumstances, are entitled to an efficient, full-time education which is suitable to their age, ability, aptitude, and any special educational needs they may have.

Children missing education are at significant risk of underachieving, being victims of harm, exploitation, or radicalisation, and becoming NEET (not in education, employment, or training) later in life.

Effective information sharing between parents, schools, and local authorities is critical to ensuring that all children of compulsory school age are safe and receiving suitable education.

Attendance, absence, and exclusions are closely monitored. The DSLs will monitor unauthorised absences and take appropriate action, particularly where children go missing on repeated occasions and/or are missing for periods during the school day.

Where a pupil has 10 consecutive school days of unexplained absence and all reasonable steps\* have been taken by the school to establish their whereabouts without success, the school will make an immediate referral to the appropriate local social services.

*Reasonable steps include:*

*   Telephone calls to all known contacts
*   E-mails to relatives
*   Contact with other schools where siblings may be registered
*   Enquiries to friends, neighbours etc. through school contacts
*   Enquiries with any other service known to be involved with the pupil/family
*   Possible home visits where safe to do so
*   All contacts and outcomes to be recorded on the pupil's file

The school is required by law to have an admission register and an attendance register. All pupils must be placed on both registers.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.11.L0: Recognising Abuse and Categories]
1.  **Recognising Abuse:**
    To ensure that our pupils are protected from harm, we need to understand what types of behaviour constitute abuse and neglect. Abuse and neglect are forms of maltreatment. Somebody may abuse or neglect a child by inflicting harm, for example, by hitting them, or by failing to act to prevent harm, for example, by leaving a small child home alone or leaving knives or matches within reach of an unattended toddler. Abuse may be committed by adult men or women and by other children and young people. There are four categories of abuse: physical abuse, emotional abuse, sexual abuse, and neglect.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.11.L3: Physical Abuse Definition]
2.  **Physical Abuse:**
    Physical abuse is a form of abuse which may involve hitting, shaking, throwing, poisoning, burning or scalding, drowning, suffocating, or otherwise causing physical harm to a child. Physical harm may also be caused when a parent or carer fabricates the symptoms of, or deliberately induces, illness in a child (this used to be called Munchausen's Syndrome by Proxy but is now more usually referred to as fabricated or induced illness).

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.11.L21: Indicators of Abuse and Reporting]
B. Indicators of Abuse:**
    Physical signs define some types of abuse, for example, bruising, bleeding, or broken bones resulting from physical or sexual abuse, or injuries sustained while a child has been inadequately supervised. The identification of physical signs is complicated, as children may go to great lengths to hide injuries, often because they are ashamed or embarrassed, or their abuser has threatened further violence or trauma if they 'tell'. It is also quite difficult for anyone without medical training to categorise injuries into accidental or deliberate with any degree of certainty. For those reasons, it is vital that staff are also aware of the range of behavioural indicators of abuse and report any concerns to the Designated Safeguarding Lead.

    It is the responsibility of staff to report their concerns. It is not their responsibility to investigate or decide whether a child has been abused.

    A child who is being abused or neglected may:

    *   Have bruises, bleeding, burns, fractures, or other injuries.
    *   Show signs of pain or discomfort.
    *   Keep arms and legs covered, even in warm weather.
    *   Be concerned about changing for PE or swimming.
    *   Look unkempt and uncared for.
    *   Change their eating habits.
    *   Have difficulty in making or sustaining friendships.
    *   Appear fearful.
    *   Be reckless with regard to their own or others' safety.
    *   Self-harm.
    *   Frequently miss school or arrive late.
    *   Show signs of not wanting to go home.
    *   Display a change in behaviour - from quiet to aggressive, or happy-go-lucky to withdrawn.
    *   Challenge authority.
    *   Become disinterested in their schoolwork.
    *   Be constantly tired or preoccupied.
    *   Be wary of physical contact.
    *   Be involved in, or particularly knowledgeable about, drugs or alcohol; and/or display sexual knowledge or behaviour beyond that normally expected for their age and/or stage of development.
    *   Acquire gifts such as money or a mobile phone from new 'friends' or adults recently acquainted with the child's family.

    Individual indicators will rarely, in isolation, provide conclusive evidence of abuse. They should be viewed as part of a jigsaw, and each small piece of information will help the DSL to decide how to proceed.

    It is very important that staff report all of their concerns, however minor or insignificant they may think they are; they do not need 'absolute proof' that the child is at risk.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.13: Responding to child abuse disclosures]
Any child in any family in any school could become a victim of abuse. Staff should always maintain an attitude of "It could happen here".

1.  **If a pupil discloses to a member of staff or volunteer:**
    It takes a lot of courage for a child to disclose that they are being abused. They may feel ashamed, particularly if the abuse is sexual. Their abuser may have threatened what will happen if they tell. They may have lost all trust in adults. Or they may believe, or have been told, that the abuse is their own fault. Sometimes they may not be aware that what is happening is abusive.

    If a pupil talks to a member of staff about any risks to their safety or wellbeing, the staff member will need to let the pupil know that they must pass the information on - staff are not allowed to keep secrets. The point at which they tell the pupil this is a matter of professional judgment. If they jump in immediately, the pupil may think that they do not want to listen, but if left until the very end of the conversation, the pupil may feel that they have been misled into revealing more than they would have otherwise.

    The pupils who have been completing the Protective Behaviours curriculum will have been taught about confidentiality and will generally understand the concept of safe and unsafe secrets. They should have a good knowledge and understanding of why staff cannot keep some information confidential but also know that information is passed on to specific people on a 'need to know' basis only.

    During their conversations with pupils, staff will:
    *   Allow them to speak freely.
    *   Remain calm and not overreact - the pupil may stop talking if they feel they are upsetting their listener.
    *   Give reassuring nods or words of comfort - 'I'm so sorry this has happened', 'I want to help', 'This isn't your fault', 'You are doing the right thing in talking to me'.
    *   Not be afraid of silences - staff must remember how hard this must be for the pupil.
    *   Under no circumstances ask investigative questions - such as how many times this has happened, whether it happens to siblings too, or what does the pupil's mother think about all this (however, it is reasonable to ask questions to clarify understanding and to support a meaningful referral if that is required, e.g., when did this happen, where did this happen?).
    *   At an appropriate time, tell the pupil that in order to help them, the member of staff must pass the information on.
    *   Not automatically offer any physical touch as comfort. It may be anything but comforting to a child who has been abused.
    *   Avoid admonishing the child for not disclosing earlier. Saying things such as 'I do wish you had told me about this when it started,' or 'I can't believe what I'm hearing' may be the staff member's way of being supportive but may be interpreted by the child to mean that they have done something wrong.
    *   Tell the pupil what will happen next.
    *   Let them know that someone (either you or another named person, e.g., the DSL) will come to see them before the end of the day.
    *   Report verbally to the DSL.
    *   Seek support if they feel distressed or need to debrief.

2.  **Notifying Parents:**
    The school will normally seek to discuss any concerns about a pupil with their parents. This must be handled sensitively, and the DSL will make contact with the parent in the event of a concern, suspicion, or disclosure.

    However, if the school believes that notifying parents could increase the risk to the child or exacerbate the problem, advice will be sought first from Children's Social Care.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:1.16: Standards for Effective Child Protection Practice]
**Standards for Effective Child Protection Practice in Schools**

The school's child protection and safeguarding responsibilities are inspected under the ‘Quality of Leadership and Management' and 'Behaviour and Safety' judgements in Ofsted inspections. The following standards may assist schools in evaluating their practice. They should be used jointly by the Designated Safeguarding Lead to ensure the school is effective in safeguarding and child protection matters.

**In best practice, schools:**

1.  Have an ethos in which children feel secure, their viewpoints are valued, and they are encouraged to talk and are listened to.
2.  Provide suitable support and guidance so that pupils have a range of appropriate adults to whom they can turn if they are worried or in difficulties.
3.  Work with parents to build an understanding of the school's responsibilities to safeguard and promote the welfare of all children and a recognition that this may occasionally require children to be referred to investigative agencies as a constructive and helpful measure.
4.  Ensure all staff are able to identify children who may benefit from early help; provide coordinated offers of early help; and ensure that children receive the right help at the right time to address concerns and risks and prevent issues from escalating.
5.  Are vigilant in cases of suspected child abuse, recognising the signs and symptoms, have clear procedures whereby all members of staff report such cases to the Designated Safeguarding Lead or - in her/his absence - the deputy Designated Safeguarding Lead, and are aware of Local Authority and Local Safeguarding Children Board procedures so that information is passed on effectively to the relevant professionals.
6.  Monitor children who have been identified as in need of early help or at risk; maintain clear records of pupils' progress and welfare *in a secure place*; maintain sound policies on confidentiality; provide appropriate information to other professionals; and submit reports to and attend child protection conferences.
7.  Provide and support regular child protection training and updates for all school staff and ensure that Designated Safeguarding Leads attend refresher training every two years to ensure their skills and expertise are up to date; and ensure that targeted funding for this work is used solely for this purpose.
8.  Contribute to an inter-agency approach to safeguarding and child protection by developing effective and supportive liaison with other agencies.
9.  Use the curriculum to teach children about safeguarding and raise their awareness and build confidence so that pupils have a range of contacts and strategies to ensure their own protection and understand the importance of protecting others.
10. Provide clear policy statements for parents, staff, and children and young people on the school’s policies.
11. Have a clear understanding of the various types of bullying - face-to-face, online, physical, verbal, and indirect - and act promptly and firmly to combat it, making sure that pupils are aware of the school's position on this issue and who they can contact for support.
12. Take particular care that pupils with SEN in mainstream and special schools, who may be especially vulnerable to abuse, are supported effectively, with particular attention paid to ensuring that those with communication difficulties are enabled to express themselves to a member of staff with appropriate communication skills.
13. Have a clear policy about the handling of allegations of abuse by members of staff, ensuring that all staff are fully aware of the procedures and that they are followed correctly at all times, using the guidance set out in *Keeping Children Safe in Education 2024* and WSCB inter-agency child protection procedures.
14. Have a written whole school policy, which is produced, owned, and regularly reviewed by all school staff, considering the views of children and parents/carers, and which clearly outlines the school's position and positive action in respect of the aforementioned standards.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:2.3: Confidentiality Privacy Data Protection Principles]
1.  The school is committed to maintaining confidentiality and respecting privacy in all our operations.
2.  All staff members must adhere to the principles of confidentiality and data protection legislation to safeguard the privacy of personal and sensitive information.
3.  Information should only be shared on a “need-to-know” basis and in accordance with legal obligations.
4.  Consent from parents or guardians will be sought before sharing any sensitive information with external parties, unless otherwise required by law.

All staff are trained in understanding the need to treat information given to us by parents/carers as confidential unless otherwise stated. We never give out names and addresses of members of the school community to other parents or anyone else without gaining written permission. Any documents relating to children or staff holding personal information and naming them are in files kept in the school office or under systems with user and password protection.

---

[Section en_policy1_child_protection:2.10: Confidentiality Policy Reference Documents]
*   Data Protection Legislation (GDPR)
*   Safeguarding and Child Protection Guidelines
```

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.1: Health and Safety Scope and Responsibilities]
Children should have the opportunity to engage in a broad variety of activities. Health and safety measures should support this goal, ensuring their well-being without hindering their participation. It is crucial for children to learn to recognise and manage risks as part of their everyday life. When assessing and managing risks, common sense should be applied, and health and safety procedures should always be proportionate to the risks associated with each activity. Staff should receive appropriate training to ensure they can protect themselves and the children, and effectively manage any potential risks.

**Responsibility of the school**

The employer is required to have a health and safety policy in place and ensure the necessary measures are implemented to enforce it. This is regulated under the Law 31/1995 on the *Prevention of Occupational Risks*. Employers are obligated to assess the risks associated with all activities, adopt measures to manage those risks, and communicate these measures to their employees. Furthermore, the organisation of preventive activities is governed by the Regulation of Prevention Services (*Royal Decree 39/1997*).

In practice, employers may delegate specific health and safety tasks to designated individuals. However, ultimate responsibility always remains with the employer, regardless of who carries out the tasks.

It is therefore essential for the employer to maintain a clear system of documentation and monitoring to ensure there is an audit trail. This should specify who is responsible for each task and confirm that these tasks are being performed appropriately.

**Responsibility of the employees**

At Imagine Montessori School, responsibility for health and safety is shared across different roles. To manage health and safety effectively, the following responsibilities are defined:

*   **Policy Definition and Oversight:** Specific areas are assigned to designated individuals, such as the Responsibility for Workplace Safety (HR Responsible) and oversight of Buildings and Facilities (Facility Manager).
*   **Implementation:** The practical application of health and safety measures is managed by the Head Teachers and the Head of Administration within their respective domains.

This collaborative structure ensures that all areas of the school’s operations maintain the highest standards of safety and compliance.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.3.s6: Vehicle Movements, Maintenance, Accidents, Emergencies]
N. On site vehicle movements:**

Not applicable

**O. Maintenance and when necessary, examination and test of plant and equipment such as electrical equipment, local exhaust ventilation, pressure systems, gas appliances, lifting equipment and glazing safety**

The school would identify the hazard or suspected hazard and use an external contractor.

**P. Recording and reporting accidents to staff, pupils and visitors - including those reportable under the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 1995 (RIDDOR).**

The school would record any of the above.

**Q. Fire safety, including testing of alarms and evacuation procedures**

See separate policy: Fire Safety Policy

**R. Dealing with health and safety emergencies- procedures and contacts**

By having policies, guidance and training for all the above, the school can deal with any emergencies using the correct procedures and contacts.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.4: First Aid, Accidents, Medical, Hygiene]
All Imagine’s staff have qualified first-aider status and are trained on an annual recurrent basis. Therefore, we have well above the required ratio of first aiders to pupils. For minor accidents, they will refer any accidents or any other medical conditions to the designated member of staff: Secretary. For medical emergencies, the person who finds the child/adult will deal with it immediately. Only trained first aiders administer first aid.

There is always more than one designated first-aider on the site of the school. All first aiders attend training courses biannually. First aid equipment is in the designated first aid cupboard, which is kept in the admin staff room and bathroom.

All accidents and other medical emergencies should be recorded appropriately in an accident report form. Parents /guardians should be notified and asked to sign the record sheet and a record kept. In the case of students using our bus service, parents will be notified by Phidias and we expect an acknowledgment. The accident report will be kept both on paper and in digital format at the Secretary.

Based on the information from accident reports, risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to implement mitigation actions for recurring potential risks.

If the accident is serious, the head teacher should be made aware and immediate action taken to ensure the location of the accident is still safe to use. Necessary action should be taken and where possible details recorded for an accident investigation. If members of the public are involved, names and addresses should be taken (including any witnesses).

Members of staff should be made aware of any hazards in the subjects that they teach or the activities that they supervise and should have appropriate training and access to first aid kits.

Pupils with medical conditions, such as epilepsy, asthma or severe allergies should be identified. All staff should be aware of their conditions and alert to the need for prompt action. The Secretary administers medication and is witnessed by another member of staff. A written record is kept and signed by the two members of staff. Medication is kept in the office or the refrigerator in Reception. A signed consent form is needed from the parent /guardian before prescribed medication is administered.

Staff should be aware of what levels of injury can be treated on-site and what levels automatically trigger an emergency ambulance call or visit to the hospital.

There are hygiene procedures in place in case of spillage of blood or body fluid: Employees must wear disposable gloves and aprons when cleaning any bodily fluids (spillages of blood, vomit, urine and excreta). Other persons should be kept away from the contamination until it is effectively dealt with.

The following action should be taken in the event of spillages:

*   Soak up the spillages using paper towels
*   Clean the area with hot water containing soap or detergent
*   Clean the area with a bleach or disinfectant solution - use as directed by the manufacturer.
*   'Solids’ should be carefully flushed down the toilet. Dressings, gloves, and aprons should be carefully disposed of in sealed plastic bags as clinical waste.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.7: Valencian Community Health Safety Reference Documents]
The main legislation covering health and safety in educational settings in the Valencian Community includes the Decreto 253/2019, de 29 de noviembre (DOGV), which regulates the organisation and functioning of public centres providing Early Childhood and Primary Education. This decree establishes guidelines for comprehensive safety within schools, covering aspects related to infrastructure, emergency measures, and protocols for action.

The Ley 10/2014, de 29 de diciembre, de Salud de la Comunitat Valenciana, specifies that the competent authorities, in collaboration with the educational and child protection departments, will develop educational and training programmes to promote healthy habits and prevent diseases in the school environment.

The Ley 1/1994, de 28 de marzo, de Salud Escolar, also establishes actions related to health in schools, with a strong emphasis on promoting health and preventing diseases among the student population.

In addition, the Ley 31/1995 (Occupational Risk Prevention Act) and the Reglamento de los Servicios de Prevención (Regulation of Prevention Services) apply to the educational environment, ensuring that risks are identified, managed, and mitigated. Employees also have a duty to ensure their own and others' health and safety. It is rare for school staff to face prosecution under criminal law regarding accidents involving children, as responsibility is generally considered under civil law, with a standard of care similar to that of a prudent parent.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.1: Whistleblowing Channel Policy Scope]
This policy is intended for employees, clients, suppliers, and third parties involved in the Company's operations, who may use the Whistleblowing Channel as detailed herein.

All employees (regardless of the legal form of their employment or service relationship), suppliers, sponsors, collaborators, agents, and members of the Company's various collegiate bodies are required to report any irregularity or breach within their objective scope through the Whistleblowing Channel, without fear of dismissal or other forms of retaliation. The information provided will be treated with utmost confidentiality.

The Whistleblowing Channel is intended as an instrument for reporting irregularities or breaches. Therefore, it should not be used indiscriminately but solely for the purposes for which it is designed.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.3: Whistleblowing Channel Policy Principles]
This policy has been developed by Zubi Education, SL (hereinafter referred to as "the Company"), to maintain group-wide consistency of action. Its objective is to outline the procedure followed within the internal whistleblowing channel, in compliance with various regulatory provisions, including Article 8 of Directive (EU) 2019/1937 of the European Parliament and Council dated 23 October 2019, regarding the protection of individuals who report breaches of EU law.

The objective of the Whistleblowing Channel is to enable employees, clients, suppliers, and third parties associated with the Company or its operations to report any incident within the Company that could represent a breach, offence, or violation of our internal procedures, among others:

*   Breach of Anti-Money Laundering (AML) regulations
*   Breach of Data Protection regulations
*   Breach of Market Abuse regulations
*   Breach of comprehensive sexual freedom assurance regulations
*   Breach of regulations ensuring effective equality between men and women
*   Internal Code of Conduct

The Company’s Whistleblowing Channel is accessible through the corporate website: , and is available to individuals aware of potential administrative and/or serious criminal risks attributable to any person or entity linked to the group in their professional capacity.

This Channel enables secure communication, ensuring that privacy and confidentiality are maintained throughout the process. Reports may be submitted anonymously if the whistleblower so chooses.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.5: Whistleblower Channel Communication Guidelines]
The public whistleblowing channel (“canal de denuncias”) on our website should be used as the preferred method for submitting information, as it ensures confidential data processing in accordance with the Data Protection Act.

The "Whistleblower Channel" is available on our website and is accessible to all individuals who have a relationship with us, including clients, employees, suppliers, partners, or any other parties connected to our activities. This channel allows the reporting of irregularities or inappropriate behavior, ensuring confidentiality and the right to the presumption of innocence for all involved parties. The information received through this channel will be handled with the utmost seriousness and in compliance with personal data protection regulations, ensuring that investigation procedures are conducted in accordance with applicable laws.

As a general rule, the maximum time to respond to investigation actions shall not exceed three months from the receipt of the report or, if no acknowledgment of receipt was sent to the informant, three months from the expiration of the seven-day period following the submission of the report, except in cases of special complexity that require an extension of the period, in which case it may be extended by a maximum of an additional three months.

At various stages of the processing procedure, there is provision for maintaining communication with the informant and, if deemed necessary, requesting additional information from the informant, unless they have waived this option.

In all cases, the right to the presumption of innocence and the honour of the affected parties shall be respected, as well as the provisions on the protection of personal data as stipulated.

If the reported facts are potentially indicative of a crime, the information will be referred immediately to the Public Prosecutor’s Office. Should the facts affect the financial interests of the European Union, they will be referred to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.8: Whistleblowing Report Admission Procedure]
Once the information is registered, the System Administrator will verify whether it presents facts or behaviours that fall within the scope of Annex I. They will also determine if the report needs to be reassigned to another person or management team due to any of the following circumstances:

1.  Conflict of interest of the System Administrator, as they may be personally affected by the report.
2.  Existence of an ad hoc team responsible for managing the report based on the subject matter.

The ad hoc team for handling reports will, in this case, be composed of the Chief Officer People of Zubi Group, ensuring impartiality in the process.

Following this preliminary analysis, a decision will be made within a maximum period of ten business days from the registration date of the information:

a)  **Dismiss the report** in any of the following cases:
    1.  When the facts reported are entirely implausible.
    2.  When the facts reported do not constitute a breach of the legal framework covered under Annex I.
    3.  When the report is manifestly unfounded, or there are, in the System Administrator’s opinion, reasonable indications that it was obtained through the commission of a crime. In this latter case, in addition to dismissal, a detailed report of the facts deemed to constitute a crime will be forwarded to the Public Prosecutor’s Office.
    4.  When the report contains no new and significant information regarding breaches in comparison with a previous report for which the procedures have been concluded, unless there are new factual or legal circumstances that justify a different follow-up. In such cases, the System Administrator will issue a reasoned notification of the decision.
    The dismissal will be communicated to the informant within five business days.

b)  **Admit the report for processing**.
    The admission of the report will be communicated to the informant within seven business days, unless the informant has waived the right to receive communications from the Company’s System Administrator.

c)  **Immediately forward the information to the Public Prosecutor’s Office** if the facts may indicate a crime, or to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office if the facts affect the financial interests of the European Union.

d)  **Refer the report** to the competent authority, entity, or body for processing.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.9: Whistleblowing Investigation Process and Rights]
The investigation will encompass all actions aimed at verifying the credibility of the reported facts.

It will be ensured that the person affected by the information is informed of the report, as well as of the facts presented in a summarised form. Additionally, they will be informed of their right to submit written arguments and the handling of their personal data. However, this information may be provided at the hearing stage if prior disclosure could facilitate the concealment, destruction, or alteration of evidence.

In no case will the identity of the informant be disclosed to the affected parties, nor will they be given access to the report. During the investigation, a summary of the reported facts will be provided to the respondent. This information may also be presented at the hearing if prior disclosure could facilitate the concealment, destruction, or alteration of evidence.

Without prejudice to the right to submit written arguments, the investigation will include, whenever possible, an interview with the affected individual in which they are invited to present their version of the events and to provide any evidence they consider appropriate and relevant, all while respecting the presumption of innocence.

To ensure the right to a defence, the affected individual will have access to the case file without revealing any information that could identify the informant, and they may be heard at any point in the proceedings.

The System Administrator is obligated to maintain confidentiality regarding the information they acquire during this investigation.

All Company personnel are required to cooperate with the investigation and must comply with any requests made to provide documentation, data, or any information related to the ongoing procedures, including personal data if required.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.10: Proceedings Conclusion Decisions and Data Retention]
At the conclusion of the investigation, the System Administrator will adopt one of the following decisions:

a)  **Filing of the case**, which will be notified to the informant and, if applicable, to the affected individual. In such cases, the informant will retain the right to protection as provided by this law, unless, because of the actions carried out during the investigation phase, it is concluded that the report should have been dismissed based on one of the grounds specified in this Procedure.
b)  **Referral to the Public Prosecutor’s Office** if, despite initially finding no indication that the facts may constitute a crime, this determination changes over the course of the investigation. If the crime affects the financial interests of the European Union, the case will be referred to the European Public Prosecutor’s Office.
c)  **Transfer of all actions to the competent authority**, in accordance with the provisions of this Procedure.
d)  **Initiation of disciplinary proceedings**.

Upon conclusion of all actions, the System Administrator will retain information related to the report within the whistleblowing channel software, which will contain:

a)  Date of receipt.
b)  Classification of the report.
c)  Closure date.
d)  Actions taken.

This record will not be public, and only upon a reasoned request from the competent judicial authority, within the framework of a judicial proceeding and under its supervision, may partial or full access to the content of the record be granted.

Personal data related to received reports and internal investigations referred to in the preceding section will only be retained for as long as necessary and proportionate to comply with applicable legislation. In no case will data be retained for more than ten years.

If the investigation concludes with verified proof that the reported facts are true and involve irregular or unlawful conduct, the accused may be subject to sanctions in accordance with the applicable Collective Bargaining Agreement, labour legislation, and other civil and commercial obligations held by Company staff, executive personnel, or the Board of Directors.

The Company may also refer the matter to the relevant judicial authorities if deemed necessary.

If the accused is a third party with no employment relationship with the Company (supplier, commercial agent, or business partner), applicable sanctions will be limited to the commercial sphere (limitation of actions, unilateral termination of the contract by the Company), without prejudice to the aforementioned communication to the competent judicial authorities.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.12: Whistleblowing Report Personal Data Protection]
The processing of personal data arising from the application of this Procedure will be governed by the provisions of Article 11 of Organic Law 3/2018 of 5 December, on Personal Data Protection and the Guarantee of Digital Rights, unless the report is related to matters of anti-money laundering, counter-terrorist financing, and/or sanctions, in which case the provisions of Law 10/2010 of 28 April and its implementing regulations on anti-money laundering, counter-terrorist financing, and specific sanctions will apply. Lastly, the person who is the subject of the report will be informed of the estimated time for processing the report.

In any case, the Company, as a guarantee of confidentiality for the informant, confirms that, generally, the exercise of rights by the person under investigation may be limited due to the specific nature of the report and may only be exercised regarding their own personal data that is being processed. Under no circumstances will the rights exercised by the person under investigation include data relating to the informant. If it is not possible to adopt measures to safeguard the informant’s confidentiality when notifying the person under investigation, the informant's right to confidentiality will prevail in accordance with Article 31.2 of Law 2/2023 of 20 February, which regulates the protection of individuals who report regulatory breaches and fights against corruption.

Further information is available in our privacy policy at [https://imaginemontessori.es/politica-de-privacidad/](https://imaginemontessori.es/politica-de-privacidad/).

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.13.s9: Other reportable issues]
| Other                                                 | Section for reporting any event that cannot be classified within the present annex list. The Whistleblowing Channel Protocol stipulates the handling of reports considered false or made in bad faith.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         |

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:6.15: Whistleblowing Legal Framework References]
*   **DIRECTIVE (EU) 2019/1937 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of 23 October 2019 on the protection of persons who report breaches of Union law.
*   **Law 10/2010, of 28 April on Anti-Money Laundering**.
*   **Organic Law 3/2018, of 5 December** on the Protection of Personal Data.
*   **Law 2/2023, of 20 February on Whistleblower Protection**.
*   **Law 35/2003, of 4 November, on Collective Investment Institutions**.
*   **Organic Law 10/1995, of 23 November, the Penal Code**.
*   **Royal Legislative Decree 4/2015, of 23 October**, approving the revised text of the Securities Market Act.
*   **REGULATION (EU) 2016/679 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons concerning personal data processing and on the free movement of such data (General Data Protection Regulation).
*   **Organic Law 10/2022, of 6 September on comprehensive sexual freedom assurance**.
*   **Organic Law 3/2007, of 22 March for effective equality between men and women**.
*   **Article 18.1 of the Spanish Constitution**: “The right to honour, personal and family privacy, and one’s own image is guaranteed.”
*   **Article 4.2.e) of the Workers’ Statute**: “Workers are entitled to respect for their privacy and consideration of their dignity, including protection from verbal and physical harassment of a sexual nature and harassment based on racial or ethnic origin, religion or beliefs, disability, age, or sexual orientation.”
*   **European Parliament, Article 2 of Directive 2002/73/C of 23 September 2003**, and **Law 63/2003, of 30 December**, on fiscal, administrative, and social order measures, Article 28.2, which introduce the concept of harassment. This law defines harassment as “any unwanted conduct related to racial or ethnic origin, religion or beliefs, disabilities, age, or sexual orientation of a person, aimed at or resulting in violating dignity and creating an intimidating, humiliating, or offensive environment.”
*   **World Health Organization Constitution**: (WHO): “Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being, and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.”
*   **Art. 14 of the Occupational Health and Safety Law, right to protection from occupational risks**: “Workers have the right to effective protection in matters of safety and health at work.”
*   **Art. 15 of the Prevention Law, principles of preventive action**: “The employer will apply measures that integrate the general duty of Prevention.”
*   **Law 4/2023 for real and effective equality of trans individuals and for the guarantee of LGBT+ rights**.
synthesis.raw_response:
The school should take the following actions:

1.  **Report Concerns to DSL:** Any staff member aware of the classmate's mention of bruises should report this concern to the Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) [1.11.L21, 1.16]. It is the responsibility of staff to report concerns, not to investigate or decide if abuse has occurred [1.11.L21]. Bruises are a physical sign that defines a type of abuse [1.11.L21, 1.11.L3].
2.  **Address Unexplained Absence:** The student's absence for two weeks (10 consecutive school days) with an unreachable family falls under "Children Missing Education Procedures" [1.8]. Frequent missing of school is also an indicator of abuse [1.11.L21].
3.  **Take Reasonable Steps to Locate the Child:** The school must take "all reasonable steps" to establish the child's whereabouts. These steps include:
    *   Telephone calls to all known contacts [1.8].
    *   E-mails to relatives [1.8].
    *   Contact with other schools where siblings may be registered [1.8].
    *   Enquiries to friends, neighbours etc. through school contacts [1.8].
    *   Enquiries with any other service known to be involved with the pupil/family [1.8].
    *   Possible home visits where safe to do so [1.8].
4.  **Record All Actions:** All contacts and outcomes from these steps must be recorded on the pupil's file [1.8]. The DSL is responsible for keeping detailed written records of all concerns [1.5].
5.  **Immediate Referral to Social Services:** Since the pupil has 10 consecutive school days of unexplained absence and all reasonable steps to establish their whereabouts have been taken without success, the school must make an immediate referral to the appropriate local social services [1.8]. The DSL is responsible for referring cases of suspected abuse to Children's Social Care or the Police as appropriate [1.5].
6.  **Consider Notifying Parents:** While the school would normally seek to discuss concerns with parents, if notifying them could increase the risk to the child or exacerbate the problem, advice should be sought first from Children's Social Care [1.13]. Information sharing should be on a "need-to-know" basis and in accordance with legal obligations, and consent from parents is sought unless otherwise required by law [2.3].
7.  **Utilize Local Contacts:** The school can use local emergency contacts such as Servicios Sociales de Paterna (Children's Social Care) or the Police for guidance and action in cases of child vulnerability [1.15].
