synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy4_trips_and_outings:7: Outing Safety and Emergency Procedures]
*   **Transportation:** All transportation arrangements must meet safety regulations and be provided by licensed operators. Students must adhere to safety protocols such as seatbelt use and supervised boarding and disembarking. In case an issue should arise, a member of staff needs to contact the Head of Admin.
*   **Medical Needs:** Teachers must be aware of any medical conditions or allergies of students. A first aid kit must always be available, and at least one staff member trained in first aid must accompany the group. The first aid kit is an essential collection of medical supplies and equipment designed to provide immediate care for minor injuries and emergencies that may occur in the school environment (bandages, antiseptics, and cleaning supplies). Staff will have an updated list of all allergies and specific conditions, as well as information on medication needed during the experience. All medication will require authorization from the family through a Phidias procedure, and the medicine must be given to an adult.
    *   From Year 9 upwards, students are responsible for their own medication. However, the administration of medication needs to be logged by the responsible adult.
*   **Emergency Contact:** It is extremely important to update your contact details in Phidias. If you do not have a local phone number, please ensure that you provide us with one. Staff will have a list of emergency contacts for each student that must be on hand during the outing, prepared by the admin team.
*   **Contingency Planning:** In the event of bad weather or unforeseen circumstances, alternative arrangements must be planned, or the outing postponed/cancelled.

---

[Section en_policy4_trips_and_outings:8: Accident and Emergency Procedures]
*   **Minor Injuries:**
    *   In case of minor injuries (such as cuts or bruises), first aid will be administered by the staff member trained in first aid. Families will be notified about the incident at the end of the outing, unless further medical attention is required.
*   **Serious Injuries or Medical Emergencies:**
    *   In the event of a serious injury or medical emergency, staff will immediately call emergency services (112) and follow the instructions provided by the professionals.
    *   Family/guardians will be contacted as soon as possible by the designated staff member. A member of the school staff will accompany the child to the hospital if necessary and remain with the child until the parent/guardian arrives. (This applies also in the event of an accident abroad).
    *   If the child has a fever or any other symptom that prevents him/her from enjoying the experience, the family will be contacted to pick him/her up as soon as possible.
    *   All families must agree to the pickup commitment before authorizing the outing.
    *   A full report of the incident will be submitted to the school administration.
*   **Actions to Mitigate the Risk of Losing a Child:**
    *   What actions staff need to follow to reduce the risk of missing a child during an outing:
        *   For one-day experiences, Early Years and Primary students will wear the Imagine t-shirt, which helps adults to oversee the children.
        *   Before and during the outing, remind students to notify when they need to go to a different place (e.g., the toilet) as adults need to know where children are at all times for safety reasons. Students must always go in a minimum of two.
        *   Count children every time they are changing activities or moving from one place to another (e.g., before having lunch, after lunch, when moving from a closed space to an open area) to ensure that all children are present.
*   **Lost or Missing Child:**
    *   If a child is unaccounted for during the experience, staff will immediately initiate a search of the area while keeping other students safe.
    *   If the child is not found, a member of staff needs to phone the Headteacher and, if agreed, call the police.
    *   Parents/guardians will be informed as soon as possible, and all necessary steps will be taken to ensure the child’s safety and well-being.
    *   In case there is a child missing, these are the actions to be followed by staff once the child is found:
        *   Talk to, take care of, and comfort the child.
        *   Speak to the other children to ensure they understand why they should not leave the premises/separate from a group on an outing.
        *   The Headteacher will speak to the parents to discuss events and give an account of the incident.
        *   A detailed report should be submitted, covering: time, place, numbers of staff and children, when the child was last seen, what appeared to have happened, the duration the child was missing, how s/he appeared to have gone missing, and lessons learned for the future.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.3.s3: Off-site, Contractors, and Security]
E. Off-site visits including school-led adventure activities**

See separate policy: Trips and Outings Policy

**F. Selecting and controlling contractors**

The school will use these indicators to select and control contractors: past performance, work undertaken in other schools, membership of trade bodies, accreditation of trade bodies, general health and safety awareness, organisation and arrangements for health and safety and commitment to recognised codes of practice.

**H. School Security**

This is covered in the check of the inside and external building by the Maintenance Team. The main door is always locked. The doors that are used to access the outside areas are only opened when such areas are under supervision. At the start and end of the school day, two doors are used for children to arrive or depart, and both are continuously supervised by Imagine’s staff. All other doors remain secure and are not used during these times.

**

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.3.s6: Vehicle Movements, Maintenance, Accidents, Emergencies]
N. On site vehicle movements:**

Not applicable

**O. Maintenance and when necessary, examination and test of plant and equipment such as electrical equipment, local exhaust ventilation, pressure systems, gas appliances, lifting equipment and glazing safety**

The school would identify the hazard or suspected hazard and use an external contractor.

**P. Recording and reporting accidents to staff, pupils and visitors - including those reportable under the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 1995 (RIDDOR).**

The school would record any of the above.

**Q. Fire safety, including testing of alarms and evacuation procedures**

See separate policy: Fire Safety Policy

**R. Dealing with health and safety emergencies- procedures and contacts**

By having policies, guidance and training for all the above, the school can deal with any emergencies using the correct procedures and contacts.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.4: First Aid, Accidents, Medical, Hygiene]
All Imagine’s staff have qualified first-aider status and are trained on an annual recurrent basis. Therefore, we have well above the required ratio of first aiders to pupils. For minor accidents, they will refer any accidents or any other medical conditions to the designated member of staff: Secretary. For medical emergencies, the person who finds the child/adult will deal with it immediately. Only trained first aiders administer first aid.

There is always more than one designated first-aider on the site of the school. All first aiders attend training courses biannually. First aid equipment is in the designated first aid cupboard, which is kept in the admin staff room and bathroom.

All accidents and other medical emergencies should be recorded appropriately in an accident report form. Parents /guardians should be notified and asked to sign the record sheet and a record kept. In the case of students using our bus service, parents will be notified by Phidias and we expect an acknowledgment. The accident report will be kept both on paper and in digital format at the Secretary.

Based on the information from accident reports, risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to implement mitigation actions for recurring potential risks.

If the accident is serious, the head teacher should be made aware and immediate action taken to ensure the location of the accident is still safe to use. Necessary action should be taken and where possible details recorded for an accident investigation. If members of the public are involved, names and addresses should be taken (including any witnesses).

Members of staff should be made aware of any hazards in the subjects that they teach or the activities that they supervise and should have appropriate training and access to first aid kits.

Pupils with medical conditions, such as epilepsy, asthma or severe allergies should be identified. All staff should be aware of their conditions and alert to the need for prompt action. The Secretary administers medication and is witnessed by another member of staff. A written record is kept and signed by the two members of staff. Medication is kept in the office or the refrigerator in Reception. A signed consent form is needed from the parent /guardian before prescribed medication is administered.

Staff should be aware of what levels of injury can be treated on-site and what levels automatically trigger an emergency ambulance call or visit to the hospital.

There are hygiene procedures in place in case of spillage of blood or body fluid: Employees must wear disposable gloves and aprons when cleaning any bodily fluids (spillages of blood, vomit, urine and excreta). Other persons should be kept away from the contamination until it is effectively dealt with.

The following action should be taken in the event of spillages:

*   Soak up the spillages using paper towels
*   Clean the area with hot water containing soap or detergent
*   Clean the area with a bleach or disinfectant solution - use as directed by the manufacturer.
*   'Solids’ should be carefully flushed down the toilet. Dressings, gloves, and aprons should be carefully disposed of in sealed plastic bags as clinical waste.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.5: Wasp Sting Protocol for Students]
**1. Initial assessment and safety**

*   Immediately move the student away from the area where the wasps are present to prevent further stings.
*   Check for additional wasps nearby before intervening.

**2. Assess severity**

*   Ask and observe:
    *   Single sting or multiple stings?
    *   Are you known to be allergic to insect stings?
    *   Initial symptoms:
        *   Mild: pain, redness, localised swelling.
        *   Emergency: difficulty breathing, swelling of the face/neck, widespread hives, dizziness, vomiting.

**3. Immediate action according to the situation**

**A. Mild local reaction (most common)**

*   Remove the sting if present (wasps usually do not leave one, but check).
*   Wash the area with soap and water.
*   Apply a cold compress (ice wrapped in a cloth) for 10–15 minutes to reduce swelling and pain.
*   Elevate the affected area if possible (e.g. an arm).
*   Observe the student for at least 30 minutes to rule out delayed reactions.

**B. Severe reaction (anaphylaxis) – EMERGENCY**

Warning symptoms:

*   Swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat.
*   Shortness of breath or a feeling of tightness in the chest.
*   Severe dizziness or loss of consciousness.
*   Generalised urticaria.
*   Persistent vomiting.

**Action:**

*   Call 112 immediately (state: *“possible anaphylactic reaction due to sting”*).
*   Administer self-injectable adrenaline (if reported by the family and available at the school).
*   Place the child lying down with legs elevated (if conscious).
*   Do not leave the child unattended until medical assistance arrives.

**4. Information and monitoring**

*   Notify the family immediately.
*   Record the incident (time, place, symptoms, and actions taken).
*   Monitor the student for at least 1 hour, even in mild cases, as delayed reactions may occur.

**5. Prevention**

*   Check the premises before use.
*   Inform staff to act quickly but calmly.
*   Ensure a first aid kit with instant cold packs and basic materials is available.
*   Keep a list of students with known allergies and the location of their auto-injectors.

**6. Training of staff in the use of an EpiPen**

**How to use the EpiPen:**

1.  Remove the safety cap.
2.  Hold firmly and place against the outer thigh (it can be applied over clothing).
3.  Press until you hear a click, hold for 5–10 seconds.
4.  Massage the area gently.

*   After use: call 112 and notify the family.
*   Ensure the child is taken to hospital, even if symptoms improve.
*   School Emergency Kit:
    *   Instant cold packs (for mild stings).
    *   Oral antihistamine (only if prescribed for the student).
    *   Gauze, gloves, disinfectant.
    *   List of emergency telephone numbers (112, family contacts).
    *   List of students with allergies and the location of auto-injectors (if applicable).

synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy2_technology:3: Technology Use Policy and Principles]
*   **Safe and Responsible Use:** All users of mobile devices and technology must act responsibly and professionally while using personal or school-issued devices. This includes ensuring that mobile devices are used in accordance with the school’s safeguarding policies and do not compromise the safety or well-being of the students or staff.
*   **Confidentiality and Data Protection:** The school is committed to protecting the privacy and confidentiality of all individuals. All personal data, including mobile numbers and any other confidential information, must be securely stored and handled in compliance with data protection laws (GDPR and LOPDGDD).
*   **Respect for Privacy:** The school enforces strict guidelines to prevent unauthorized photography or recording of pupils. Only authorized school personnel, with explicit parental consent, may take images of pupils for educational purposes.
*   **Security of Devices:** Personal and school-issued devices must be secured with passwords or PINs to prevent unauthorized access. Devices should be kept secure at all times, and if lost or stolen, immediate reporting to the school authorities is required.
*   **Sanctions for Misuse:** The school will take appropriate disciplinary action if mobile devices are misused. This may include confiscation of the device, parental notification, and in serious cases, police involvement.
*   **Educational Purpose:** The use of mobile devices should be restricted to educational and communication purposes, in line with school protocols. Unauthorized usage, especially for personal reasons or disruptive behaviour, will be subject to sanctions.

---

[Section en_policy2_technology:4: Technology Policy Stakeholder Roles Responsibilities]
This policy applies to all staff, pupils, parents, volunteers, and visitors.

*   **Specific Responsibilities:**
    *   **Staff:** Enforce the policy, ensuring safeguarding and data protection practices are observed. Staff are required to secure personal and school-issued devices with passwords or PINs.
    *   **Pupils:** Must secure devices with passwords to prevent unauthorised access. They should only use devices as permitted and respect school protocols.
    *   **Parents/Visitors:** Follow the school’s mobile and device policy; taking photographs or recordings of pupils without explicit consent is prohibited.

---

[Section en_policy2_technology:general-policy-for-mobil: Mobile Use Policy: Parents, Visitors, Volunteers]
*   **On School Grounds:** Parents, visitors, and volunteers must adhere to staff guidelines regarding device use, including prohibitions on unauthorized recordings or photos of pupils, and refraining from using mobiles where pupils are present.
*   **School Trips:** Parents or volunteers assisting on school trips or events must not use mobiles to contact other parents or take photos of pupils, their work, or any other potentially identifying information.
*   **School Performances:** Parents are permitted to use their personal mobiles during school festivities, at their own responsibility.

---

[Section en_policy2_technology:10: School Camera Mobile Device Policy]
The school’s policy on mobile phones and cameras aligns with safeguarding protocols to protect pupils’ privacy and safety.

*   **Parental Photography:** Parents are prohibited from photographing or recording pupils during events unless specified by the school (e.g., end-of-year celebrations).
*   **Internet Filtering and Monitoring:** The school collaborates with internet service providers to ensure effective filtering. Any inappropriate content discovered is reported to the Headteachers and reviewed regularly to maintain safety standards.

---

[Section en_family_manual_24_25:24: Student Photos and Media Policy]
At Imagine, children work in shared spaces and often in teams. While working, their guides take photographs and videos to document their progress, with many of them featuring several children together. Therefore, it is part of our policy that all families at the centre give their consent to share images of their children with other families in the school.

Additionally, we occasionally share content in the media (press, our website, or social media). For this, a specific authorization will be required, which you should sign if you give your consent.

As we take this matter very seriously, it is possible that the total number of photos or videos of children whose images are not authorized for media use will be fewer than those with full permissions.

**Annual Yearbook:** During the school year, a professional photographer will take individual and group photos of all our children. We will provide digital copies to all families at an additional cost, which will be charged using the usual method. We will notify you in advance of the date on which the photo session will take place.

Given the variety of preferences among families regarding different formats for their various uses—photo albums (digital or physical), sharing with distant relatives and friends, multiple copies in different sizes, etc.—and following our sustainability principles to avoid printing photos that might not be used, we provide them digitally so that families can decide how to use them, either digitally or in print.

synthesis.sections_text:
[Section en_policy4_trips_and_outings:5: Outing Planning and Preparation]
*   **Approval Process:** All experiences must be approved by the pedagogical coordinator to ensure they meet educational objectives, logistical feasibility, and safety standards.
*   **Risk Assessments:** A thorough risk assessment must be completed for every outing to identify potential risks and implement necessary safety measures.
*   **Itinerary and Objectives:** A clear itinerary and learning objectives for each outing must be provided to parents and relevant staff prior to the trip.
*   **Family Consent:** Family consent is required for all students attending school outings. Consent forms must detail the purpose of the outing, location, itinerary, transportation, and any associated costs. This is a procedure shared with families through Phidias.

---

[Section en_policy4_trips_and_outings:6: Outing Supervision and Staff Roles]
*   **Staff Supervision:** Staff supervision is required for all outings. An appropriate staff-to-student ratio, based on the age of the students and the nature of the trip, must be maintained. All staff members must read the risk assessment and are responsible for the safety, behaviour, and well-being of students throughout the outing.
*   **External Providers:** If external guides or instructors are involved, they must be vetted by the school, and their qualifications must align with the school's standards for student safety.

---

[Section en_policy4_trips_and_outings:7: Outing Safety and Emergency Procedures]
*   **Transportation:** All transportation arrangements must meet safety regulations and be provided by licensed operators. Students must adhere to safety protocols such as seatbelt use and supervised boarding and disembarking. In case an issue should arise, a member of staff needs to contact the Head of Admin.
*   **Medical Needs:** Teachers must be aware of any medical conditions or allergies of students. A first aid kit must always be available, and at least one staff member trained in first aid must accompany the group. The first aid kit is an essential collection of medical supplies and equipment designed to provide immediate care for minor injuries and emergencies that may occur in the school environment (bandages, antiseptics, and cleaning supplies). Staff will have an updated list of all allergies and specific conditions, as well as information on medication needed during the experience. All medication will require authorization from the family through a Phidias procedure, and the medicine must be given to an adult.
    *   From Year 9 upwards, students are responsible for their own medication. However, the administration of medication needs to be logged by the responsible adult.
*   **Emergency Contact:** It is extremely important to update your contact details in Phidias. If you do not have a local phone number, please ensure that you provide us with one. Staff will have a list of emergency contacts for each student that must be on hand during the outing, prepared by the admin team.
*   **Contingency Planning:** In the event of bad weather or unforeseen circumstances, alternative arrangements must be planned, or the outing postponed/cancelled.

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.3.s3: Off-site, Contractors, and Security]
E. Off-site visits including school-led adventure activities**

See separate policy: Trips and Outings Policy

**F. Selecting and controlling contractors**

The school will use these indicators to select and control contractors: past performance, work undertaken in other schools, membership of trade bodies, accreditation of trade bodies, general health and safety awareness, organisation and arrangements for health and safety and commitment to recognised codes of practice.

**H. School Security**

This is covered in the check of the inside and external building by the Maintenance Team. The main door is always locked. The doors that are used to access the outside areas are only opened when such areas are under supervision. At the start and end of the school day, two doors are used for children to arrive or depart, and both are continuously supervised by Imagine’s staff. All other doors remain secure and are not used during these times.

**

---

[Section en_policy3_health_safety_reporting:2.4: First Aid, Accidents, Medical, Hygiene]
All Imagine’s staff have qualified first-aider status and are trained on an annual recurrent basis. Therefore, we have well above the required ratio of first aiders to pupils. For minor accidents, they will refer any accidents or any other medical conditions to the designated member of staff: Secretary. For medical emergencies, the person who finds the child/adult will deal with it immediately. Only trained first aiders administer first aid.

There is always more than one designated first-aider on the site of the school. All first aiders attend training courses biannually. First aid equipment is in the designated first aid cupboard, which is kept in the admin staff room and bathroom.

All accidents and other medical emergencies should be recorded appropriately in an accident report form. Parents /guardians should be notified and asked to sign the record sheet and a record kept. In the case of students using our bus service, parents will be notified by Phidias and we expect an acknowledgment. The accident report will be kept both on paper and in digital format at the Secretary.

Based on the information from accident reports, risk analyses are periodically conducted using multivariable analysis to implement mitigation actions for recurring potential risks.

If the accident is serious, the head teacher should be made aware and immediate action taken to ensure the location of the accident is still safe to use. Necessary action should be taken and where possible details recorded for an accident investigation. If members of the public are involved, names and addresses should be taken (including any witnesses).

Members of staff should be made aware of any hazards in the subjects that they teach or the activities that they supervise and should have appropriate training and access to first aid kits.

Pupils with medical conditions, such as epilepsy, asthma or severe allergies should be identified. All staff should be aware of their conditions and alert to the need for prompt action. The Secretary administers medication and is witnessed by another member of staff. A written record is kept and signed by the two members of staff. Medication is kept in the office or the refrigerator in Reception. A signed consent form is needed from the parent /guardian before prescribed medication is administered.

Staff should be aware of what levels of injury can be treated on-site and what levels automatically trigger an emergency ambulance call or visit to the hospital.

There are hygiene procedures in place in case of spillage of blood or body fluid: Employees must wear disposable gloves and aprons when cleaning any bodily fluids (spillages of blood, vomit, urine and excreta). Other persons should be kept away from the contamination until it is effectively dealt with.

The following action should be taken in the event of spillages:

*   Soak up the spillages using paper towels
*   Clean the area with hot water containing soap or detergent
*   Clean the area with a bleach or disinfectant solution - use as directed by the manufacturer.
*   'Solids’ should be carefully flushed down the toilet. Dressings, gloves, and aprons should be carefully disposed of in sealed plastic bags as clinical waste.
